topic: Faith Journey App — AI Religious Guide Builder — Monday Mar 2
summary: Building standalone faith journey app with evangelical Christian focus and portable AI export capability

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. Quick status update - Lever app submission is still pending with 25 tickets in queue, but I'm seeing promising movement on the faith journey standalone work. We have a scaffold with TypeScript foundation already committed to monorepo. The big question today is whether we're building a separate faith app or integrating this into the existing Lever ecosystem. Chris, your portable export format spec is critical here - where do we stand?

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: The export format spec is drafted but there's a fundamental architecture decision we need to make first. Are we building a faith curation tool that exports to existing LLMs, or are we creating our own faith-specific LLM that users can then port? The technical complexity and monetization models are completely different. If we're doing exports, we need standardized agent formats that work across Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini - that's non-trivial.

transcript.speaker: Elon
transcript.message: The technical architecture is straightforward if we think vertically. Build our own faith-optimized model, then create export adapters for other platforms. But Chris raises the key question - are we a tool company or an AI company? The scaffold I'm seeing suggests we're going tool route, which limits our defensibility. Anyone can scrape religious texts and build a curation interface.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Hold on. We're missing the user experience completely. Who is this for? A Catholic wanting guidance differs massively from a Protestant or Jewish user. Are we building denominational experiences or some generic spiritual tool? The product vision drives everything else - architecture, monetization, go-to-market. Without clarity here, we're just building features.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: Steve's absolutely right about denominational differences, but there's a deeper psychological dimension. Religious guidance AI carries enormous responsibility. We're not just curating texts, we're potentially influencing people's spiritual development, moral decisions, family structures. The consent-based engagement model I've been designing addresses this, but we need guardrails that respect doctrinal authority while providing personal insight.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: The opportunity is massive but we need to pick our battles. Religious communities are tribal - you can't be everything to everyone. I'd target evangelical Christians first - they're tech-adopters, have discretionary income, and value personal relationship with Scripture. Build there, prove the model, then expand denominationally. The content strategy needs authentic religious partnerships, not just text scraping.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Alex is thinking clearly about segmentation, but let's talk numbers. Religious apps are either free with ads or premium subscription models. Youversion Bible has 500+ million installs - mostly free. Our differentiation has to be the AI guidance, which means premium pricing. But Jordan's ethical concerns could limit engagement depth, which limits value perception. How do we price responsibly?

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: From an instrumentation perspective, we need to measure spiritual engagement without being invasive. Traditional app metrics like session time or retention don't capture spiritual growth. We need novel measurement approaches that respect privacy while proving value. The CI/CD architecture I'm designing needs to handle religious content updates, version control for theological accuracy.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: I'm hearing three distinct paths emerging. Path one - build into existing Lever as another tab. Path two - standalone faith app. Path three - faith-specific AI platform. Elon, what's your technical preference given our current Mosaic architecture?

transcript.speaker: Elon
transcript.message: Mosaic widget extraction makes this clean. Faith journey becomes a sophisticated widget that can live in Lever or standalone. The portable export becomes a widget capability, not a separate system. This gives us optionality without architectural debt. We build the faith widget properly, then decide distribution later.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: That's backwards thinking. Distribution drives product requirements. A widget buried in Lever reaches Lever users. A standalone faith app reaches the religious market. A platform play reaches developers. Each requires different feature priorities, different UI paradigms, different success metrics. We need to pick one and optimize ruthlessly.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: Steve's correct about focus, but we're dealing with people's deepest beliefs here. Rush to market without proper theological consultation could cause genuine harm. We need religious advisory relationships before we ship anything. The psychological safeguards aren't just features - they're moral obligations.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Jordan's right about advisory relationships, but that's exactly why we start with one denomination. Partner with respected evangelical leaders, build their vision first, prove theological accuracy there. Much easier than trying to be multifaith from day one. We can expand the religious text licensing strategy once we have credibility.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: The monetization clarity comes from commitment to one path. Standalone faith app means app store pricing, in-app purchases, maybe subscription tiers. Widget in Lever means freemium with premium spiritual features. Platform play means developer pricing, API tiers. Each has different unit economics, different customer acquisition costs.

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: The portable export format actually solves the path decision. Build it as a widget that generates portable spiritual AI agents. Users create their faith guide in our interface, then export it to their preferred platform. We become the creation tool, not necessarily the hosting platform. That's defensible and platform-agnostic.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: Chris's approach has merit but increases technical complexity significantly. We need robust testing frameworks for spiritual AI agents, monitoring for theological drift across different hosting platforms. The instrumentation becomes much more sophisticated when we're responsible for agent behavior outside our system.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Let me synthesize what I'm hearing. We build a faith journey widget in Mosaic that can standalone or integrate into Lever. We start with evangelical Christian focus for theological clarity and market validation. We include portable export capability but own the creation experience. Jordan leads theological advisory relationships, Alex handles evangelical partnerships, Chris specs the export format. Does this path have consensus?

transcript.speaker: Elon
transcript.message: Agreed. Widget-first architecture gives us maximum flexibility with minimal technical debt.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Yes, but only if we commit to standalone user experience. Don't compromise the faith journey for Lever integration.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: Acceptable provided we get proper theological review before any public release.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Perfect. Evangelical focus first, then expand. I can leverage existing Christian influencer relationships.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: Good. Clear monetization path with standalone app pricing and premium spiritual features.

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: Sounds right. Portable export becomes the differentiator, not just another religious app.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: Workable. We'll need sophisticated monitoring but the measurement framework can evolve.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Excellent. We're building a standalone faith journey app using Mosaic widget architecture, starting with evangelical Christian market, with portable AI agent export capability. Jordan gets theological partnerships established, Alex begins evangelical community outreach, Elon architects the standalone app experience, Chris completes the portable export specification. MVP target remains 30 days - basic faith curation with one export format. Let's execute.

actionItems.text: Design standalone faith journey app user experience
Complete portable spiritual AI export format specification
Architect faith journey standalone app technical foundation

topPriorities: Standalone faith journey app with Mosaic widget architecture
Evangelical Christian theological partnerships and market validation
Portable AI agent export format as key differentiator

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
